region_of_origin: Indian Subcontinent (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Sri Lanka); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Cooked legume dish — split red lentils (masoor) boiled with water and seasoning; 1 bowl = 200g cooked

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03309882 represents red lentil as an ingredient; no specific FoodOn term exists for masoor dal as a prepared dish. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lentils (cooked) — FoodEx2 category for cooked pulses/legumes; closest defensible match for cooked masoor dal
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for masoor dal as a prepared dish. A00LC (lentils, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for masoor dal as a prepared dish. Null used rather than invent a code.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one bowl of cooked red lentil dal, approximately 200g, as typically served at Indian restaurants and home kitchens in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Red Lentils (Masoor Dal) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172421) and ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for cooked red lentils (masoor dal) are drawn from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (lentils, pink or red, raw and cooked entries) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). Sodium value of 200mg/100g reflects typical home-cooked preparation with added salt; plain cooked lentils per USDA contain ~2mg/100g sodium. The elevated sodium is consistent with a prepared dal dish. All other macros and micronutrients reflect the cooked state per approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — red lentils (split, cooked); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Research and GI News)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 21 (very low) for split red lentils (cooked) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per 200g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (21 × 15.6g) / 100 = 3.3. This is consistent with published values in Atkinson et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt in cooking (200mg/100g; 400mg per 200g bowl) offset by high potassium (440mg per bowl) and dietary fibre supporting lipid management
hypertension.guidance: A 200g bowl of masoor dal contributes approximately 400mg sodium (17% of the 2,300mg daily limit), which is moderate for a prepared dish; patients with hypertension should request low-salt preparation and benefit from the high potassium-to-sodium ratio and the DASH diet recommendation of 4–5 legume servings per week for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Exceptionally low glycaemic index (GI 21) and glycaemic load (GL 3.3 per 200g bowl) with high dietary fibre (7.8g per bowl) and protein (9g per bowl) slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Masoor dal is one of the most diabetes-friendly foods available; its GI of 21 and GL of 3.3 per 200g bowl are among the lowest of any staple food, and multiple RCTs demonstrate that regular lentil consumption significantly improves HbA1c and fasting glucose in Type 2 Diabetes — replacing 150g cooked white rice (GL ~27) with one 200g bowl of masoor dal reduces meal glycaemic load by approximately 24 units.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (3.9g/100g), high potassium, and high folate (90mcg/100g) supporting cardiovascular risk reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Masoor dal is cardioprotective: its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre (which reduces LDL cholesterol), high potassium (supporting blood pressure), and high folate (reducing homocysteine levels) make it an ideal food for cardiovascular disease prevention and management, consistent with AHA dietary guidelines recommending legumes as a primary protein source.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate content (180mcg per 200g bowl) directly relevant for patients on methotrexate, which depletes folate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Masoor dal provides 180mcg folate per 200g bowl, making it one of the best dietary folate sources for RA patients on methotrexate therapy, where folate supplementation is standard protocol to mitigate methotrexate-induced folate depletion and reduce side effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red lentil dal (masoor dal) is a cooked dish made from split, dehusked red lentils (Lens culinaris var. masoor) — small, orange-red lentils that cook in 15–20 minutes without pre-soaking because their outer hull has been removed. When boiled, they collapse into a thick, smooth, creamy orange-yellow porridge-like consistency ideal for dal curries and soups. The dish is a staple of Indian, Bangladeshi, Pakistani, and Sri Lankan home cooking and is widely available at Indian restaurants, tiffin stalls, and vegetarian eateries across Singapore. Nutritionally, masoor dal holds the dual distinction of being one of the lowest-GI foods available (GI ~21, GL ~3.3 per 200g bowl) and one of the most nutritionally complete plant protein sources. A 200g bowl delivers approximately 9g protein, 7.8g dietary fibre, 180mcg folate, 3.2mg iron, and 440mg potassium, while contributing only 104 kcal. The extremely low GI results from the unique starch structure of red lentils: predominantly slowly digestible starch, soluble fibre forming a viscous gel matrix in the gut, and protein slowing gastric emptying. For Type 2 Diabetes management, replacing 150g cooked white rice (GL ~27) with one 200g bowl of masoor dal (GL ~3.3) in one meal per day reduces daily glycaemic load by approximately 24 units — a clinically meaningful reduction supported by multiple RCTs showing improved HbA1c. The high folate content (90mcg/100g) makes masoor dal particularly valuable for rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for cooked red lentils are well-documented in both USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). GI value of 21 is directly available in the University of Sydney GI Database for split red lentils (cooked). Sodium reflects typical prepared dal with added salt, documented in source notes. All core macros and key micronutrients are supported by approved primary sources.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked masoor dal as a prepared dish is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN at the level of individual fatty acid fractions for this preparation; total fat (0.8g/100g) is confirmed. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific home-cooked preparation with added salt is not reported as a discrete value in the approved sources consulted. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as masoor dal is a plant food containing no animal products — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for plant-based foods with no animal-derived ingredients — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for plain cooked lentils per USDA FoodData Central. 7. Sodium value of 200mg/100g reflects typical home-cooked preparation with added salt; plain cooked lentils per USDA contain approximately 2mg/100g sodium — the elevated value is appropriate for a prepared dal dish as described in the food record. 8. The input description references dry-weight protein of 25.1g/100g and folate of 479mcg DFE/100g — these are dry/raw values and are not applicable to the cooked per_100g values used here, which reflect the cooked state per approved sources. 9. Q8 self-check: all string values in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — Dal lentil merah corrected to Dal Lentil Merah (Masoor Dal, masak) in malay array to match flat aliases; all entries confirmed consistent.
